senses.definition: people who insist on preserving traditional rules or standards and resist change
examples: The purists argued that the original manuscript should not be altered.
We consulted the purists to ensure the ceremony followed the long-standing tradition.

senses.definition: a person who strongly dislikes pleasure and insists on moral or religious purity
examples: Some viewed him as a puritan because of his strict rules about behavior.
She avoided social gatherings, seeing it as an act of Puritan restraint.

senses.definition: a member of a historical Protestant group in 16th–17th century England and America
examples: Puritans sought to simplify church ceremonies.
The voyage carried many Puritans to the New World.

senses.definition: the state or quality of being a Puritan; strict moral or religious discipline
examples: The novel critiques puritandom as a social constraint.
Puritandom can limit personal expression in certain communities.

senses.definition: the quality or state of being a Puritan; strict or moralistic behavior
examples: Her puritaness made her skeptical of casual dating.
Puritaness in the community shaped local laws.

senses.definition: of or relating to Puritans; very strict or puritanical
examples: The puritanic rules discouraged dancing and sports.
A puritanic approach to work praised punctuality and thrift.

senses.definition: strict about morals and behavior, often overly so
examples: Her puritanical standards made socializing difficult.
The editor rejected the article for its puritanical tone.

senses.definition: in a strict, moralistic, or puritanical manner
examples: He spoke puritanically about the importance of discipline.
The school governs puritanically, banning many forms of expression.

senses.definition: the quality of being excessively moralistic or strict
examples: Her puritanicalness wore thin after years of rules.
Puritanicalness can suppress creativity.

senses.definition: to make or become puritanical or morally strict
examples: The policy puritanises industry standards.
They puritanise the public ceremonies by removing secular elements.

senses.definition: made puritanical or morally strict
examples: The festival was puritanised to remove any risque content.
Puritanised standards constrained artistic expression.

senses.definition: third-person singular present of puritanise; makes puritanical
examples: The agency puritanises its guidelines to curb excess.
They puritanises language in official communications.

senses.definition: present participle of puritanise; making something puritanical
examples: The movement is puritanising cultural life in the city.
They are puritanising the broadcast content.

senses.definition: the beliefs or practices of Puritans; strict religious or moral discipline
examples: Puritanism shaped early colonial communities.
Some critics see modern puritanism as excessive control.

senses.definition: plural of puritanism; multiple forms or expressions of puritanical belief
examples: Different puritanisms appeared across colonies.
The book compares various Puritanisms of the era.

senses.definition: to cause to become puritanical or morally strict
examples: Laws puritanize public speech.
The project aims to puritanize corporate culture.

senses.definition: made puritanical or morally strict
examples: The festival was puritanized to remove provocative content.
Puritanized standards governed the event.

senses.definition: a person or thing that puritanizes; a reformer of morals
examples: The reformer was labeled a puritanizer by opponents.
As a puritanizer, he pushed for stricter rules.

senses.definition: third-person singular present of puritanize; enforces puritanical standards
examples: The coalition puritanizes media content.
They puritanizes public discourse through policy.

senses.definition: present participle of puritanize; making something puritanical
examples: The government is puritanizing the constitution.
Puritanizing reforms affected entertainment laws.

senses.definition: like a Puritan; morally strict or austere
examples: The town had a puritanlike code of conduct.
He led a puritanlike life, avoiding excess.

senses.definition: in a puritanical or morally strict way
examples: She spoke puritanly about social etiquette.
The committee behaved puritanly, rejecting many proposals.

senses.definition: an adjective for something Puritan or a person of Puritan culture, used in some contexts; also a given name in rare cases
examples: The term puritano appeared in the historical text.
In some dialects, puritano refers to a Puritan farmer.

senses.definition: plural of Puritan; people who followed Puritan beliefs or who are morally strict
examples: The Puritans played a major role in early American colonies.
Puritans are often discussed in religious history.

senses.definition: the state of being pure; freedom from contamination
examples: The purities of the water were tested daily.
Cultural purities were challenged by global influences.

senses.definition: the state of being clean and free from dirt or contaminants; the quality of being pure
examples: Water purity is important for drinking.
The purity of the gold was confirmed by testing.

senses.definition: the degree to which something is free from foreign or mixed elements; integrity
examples: The purity of the data was checked before analysis.
He questioned the purity of the claim.
